topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I stared at the enrollment form, the ink blurring under my gaze. It was a familiar, unwelcome sight: Mrs. Albright’s name, listed under ‘Primary Educator.’ My stomach tightened into a knot, a cold, heavy pressure spreading through my chest. I reread the details, the dates, the school district—it all clicked into place with sickening finality. My son, Leo, was going to have the same teacher I had. I slammed the form onto the cluttered desk, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet living room. I paced three steps to the window, knuckles white where I gripped the frame, trying to physically shake the sudden, unwelcome wave of dread that washed over me.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered, the word catching in my throat like grit. I had been reviewing Leo’s school records, a necessary evil before the registration deadline, and there it was. Ms. Davies. The very same woman who had managed his kindergarten year, the one whose enthusiasm I’d initially admired, but whose relentless focus on rote memorization had left me utterly drained. I leaned back in my chair, letting out a slow, deliberate breath that did little to settle the churning in my gut. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my temples. I picked up the phone, my fingers hovering over the speed dial for the school office, a strange, brittle calm settling over my features as I prepared to face the inevitable conversation.

[story 3]

I pinched the bridge of my nose, the skin protesting the pressure. Another evening, another piece of administrative paperwork confirming the impossible. Leo’s new placement. The teacher. It was Ms. Albright again. I pushed the documents away with unnecessary force, the papers scattering across the floor like fallen leaves. My shoulders slumped, a heavy, defeated weight settling onto my back. I stood up abruptly, needing movement, needing to walk until the tension in my limbs began to dissipate. I walked into the kitchen, running a hand through my hair, the motion jerky and agitated. I just wanted the whole situation to vanish, to dissolve into nothingness.
